nodes.title: Define a differential equation
nodes.brief: Recognise a differential equation as one involving derivatives of a dependent variable.
nodes.quote: an equation involving derivative (derivatives) of the dependent variable with respect to independent variable (variables) is called a differential equation.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — What a differential equation is.
An equation that contains at least one derivative of the dependent variable with respect to the independent variable is called a differential equation. If all derivatives are with respect to a single independent variable, it is called an ordinary differential equation (ODE). Example: dy/dx = 2x is a differential equation because it contains the derivative dy/dx; it is ordinary because x is the only independent variable.

PART 2 — Identifying the roles of the variables.
In an ODE the independent variable is the one you differentiate with respect to (often x or t), and the dependent variable is the one whose derivative appears (often y). A purely algebraic equation such as y^2 + x = 5 is NOT a differential equation because no derivative appears. Always check: does the equation involve dy/dx, y'', or similar? If yes, it is a differential equation.

PART 3 — Classifying as ordinary.
An equation is ordinary if it has derivatives with respect to exactly one independent variable. Equations with partial derivatives (d^2u/dx^2 + d^2u/dy^2 = 0) are partial differential equations and outside Class 12 scope. Every equation in this chapter is an ODE.
keyMoves: Check the equation contains at least one derivative of the dependent variable
Identify the dependent and independent variables
Classify it as ordinary if there is a single independent variable
misconceptions: Calling an equation with no derivatives a differential equation
Confusing the dependent and independent variables

nodes.title: Find the order of a differential equation
nodes.brief: Define the order as the order of the highest-order derivative present.
nodes.quote: Order of a differential equation is defined as the order of the highest order derivative
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Definition of order.
The order of a differential equation is the order of the highest-order derivative of the dependent variable present in the equation. First derivative y' (or dy/dx) gives order 1; second derivative y'' (or d^2y/dx^2) gives order 2; and so on. Example: d^2y/dx^2 + 3 dy/dx + 2y = 0 has order 2 because the highest derivative is d^2y/dx^2.

PART 2 — Order is not affected by powers.
The power (exponent) to which a derivative is raised does NOT change the order. In (dy/dx)^3 + y = x the highest derivative is still dy/dx — order is 1, not 3. Always look at which derivative it is (1st, 2nd, ...), not how many times it is multiplied by itself.

PART 3 — Reading the order from an equation.
To find the order: list every derivative that appears, identify which has the most primes (or highest subscript), and report that number. Example: y''' - 4y' + y = 0 contains y', y'' (implied in between? no — just y''' and y') — the highest is y''' = d^3y/dx^3, so order = 3.
keyMoves: Identify every derivative appearing in the equation
Pick the one of highest order
Report that order as the order of the equation
misconceptions: Confusing order (highest derivative) with degree (highest power)
Counting the power of a derivative as the order

nodes.title: Find the degree of a differential equation
nodes.brief: Define the degree (when it exists) as the highest power of the highest-order derivative, in polynomial form.
nodes.quote: we mean the highest power (positive integral index) of the highest order derivative involved in the given differential equation.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — When degree is defined.
The degree of a differential equation is defined only when the equation can be written as a polynomial in the derivatives (y', y'', ...) — that is, derivatives appear only as terms raised to whole-number powers, not inside transcendental functions or under radicals. When this condition holds, the degree is the highest power (positive integral index) of the highest-order derivative present. Example: (d^2y/dx^2)^3 + (dy/dx)^2 + y = 0 is a polynomial in the derivatives; the highest-order derivative is d^2y/dx^2 and its power is 3, so degree = 3.

PART 2 — When degree is NOT defined.
If the highest-order derivative appears inside sin, cos, log, e^(...), or under a fractional power that cannot be cleared by raising both sides to an integer power, the degree is not defined. Example: sin(d^2y/dx^2) + y = 0 — degree not defined because d^2y/dx^2 is inside a sine. Example: dy/dx = sqrt(1 + (d^2y/dx^2)) — squaring both sides gives a polynomial, so degree IS defined (= 2 for the resulting equation after clearing the radical).

PART 3 — Distinguishing order and degree.
Order = which derivative (1st, 2nd, ...); degree = to what power that highest-order derivative is raised in the polynomial form. For (dy/dx)^4 + y = 0: order = 1 (dy/dx is first-order), degree = 4 (it is raised to the 4th power). Always find the order first, then look at the power of that derivative.
keyMoves: Check the equation is a polynomial in the derivatives
Locate the highest order derivative
Read its highest power as the degree (else degree is not defined)
misconceptions: Defining a degree for a non-polynomial-in-derivatives equation
Taking the power of a lower-order derivative as the degree

nodes.title: Recognise the general solution
nodes.brief: Identify the general solution (primitive) as the solution containing arbitrary constants.
nodes.quote: The solution which contains arbitrary constants is called the *general solution* (*primitive*) of the differential equation.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — What a solution of a DE is.
A function y = f(x) is a solution of a differential equation if, when substituted along with its derivatives into the equation, both sides are identical. For example, y = e^(2x) satisfies dy/dx = 2y because dy/dx = 2e^(2x) = 2y. A solution must make the equation true for all values of x in some interval, not just at a point.

PART 2 — The general solution and arbitrary constants.
The solution which contains arbitrary constants is called the general solution (or primitive) of the differential equation. The number of independent arbitrary constants equals the order of the equation: a first-order DE has one constant C, a second-order DE has two constants C1 and C2, and so on. Example: y = Ce^(2x) is the general solution of dy/dx = 2y (order 1, one constant C); it represents a whole family of solution curves, one for each value of C.

PART 3 — Verifying a general solution.
To verify that y = f(x, C) is the general solution of a DE: differentiate y to get dy/dx (and higher derivatives if needed), substitute both y and its derivatives into the DE, and confirm that the equation is satisfied for all values of C. Example: verify y = Ce^(2x) for dy/dx = 2y: dy/dx = 2Ce^(2x) = 2y. Confirmed.
keyMoves: Find a function that satisfies the equation
Keep the arbitrary constants in the answer
Match the number of constants to the order of the equation
misconceptions: Dropping the arbitrary constants from the general solution
Using fewer constants than the order of the equation

nodes.title: Find a particular solution from conditions
nodes.brief: Obtain a particular solution by fixing the arbitrary constants using given conditions.
nodes.quote: The solution free from arbitrary constants i.e., the solution obtained from the general solution by giving particular values to the arbitrary constants is called a *particular solution* of the differential equation.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — What a particular solution is.
A particular solution is obtained from the general solution by assigning specific numerical values to all the arbitrary constants. It therefore contains no arbitrary constants and represents a single curve from the family. The values are determined by initial or boundary conditions — extra pieces of information such as the value of y at a specific x.

PART 2 — How to find the particular solution: procedure.
Step 1: Write down the general solution (with constants). Step 2: Substitute the given condition (x = x0, y = y0) into the general solution to form an equation in the constants. Step 3: Solve for the constant(s). Step 4: Substitute the found values back. Example: general solution y = Ce^x; condition y = 3 when x = 0 gives 3 = Ce^0 = C, so C = 3, and the particular solution is y = 3e^x.

PART 3 — Particular solution from a second-order DE (two conditions needed).
For a second-order DE with general solution y = C1 f(x) + C2 g(x), two conditions are required to pin down both C1 and C2. Example: general solution y = C1 e^x + C2 e^(-x); conditions y(0) = 2 and y'(0) = 0. From y(0)=2: C1 + C2 = 2. Differentiate: y' = C1 e^x - C2 e^(-x); y'(0)=0 gives C1 - C2 = 0, so C1 = C2 = 1. Particular solution: y = e^x + e^(-x).
keyMoves: Start from the general solution with its constants
Substitute the given conditions to solve for the constants
Put the constant values back to get the particular solution
misconceptions: Leaving arbitrary constants in a particular solution
Applying conditions before finding the general solution

nodes.title: Solve a variables-separable equation
nodes.brief: When dy/dx = g(x)h(y), separate the variables and integrate both sides.
nodes.quote: the differential equation (1) is said to be of variable separable type.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Recognising a separable DE.
A differential equation dy/dx = f(x, y) is of variables-separable type if the right side can be written as a product g(x) * h(y) — a pure function of x times a pure function of y. Examples: dy/dx = x^2 * y is separable (g(x)=x^2, h(y)=y); dy/dx = x + y is NOT separable because x + y cannot be factored as g(x)*h(y).

PART 2 — The separation and integration step.
Once dy/dx = g(x) h(y) is identified, divide both sides by h(y) (assuming h(y) is not zero) and multiply both sides by dx to get dy/h(y) = g(x) dx. Then integrate both sides: integral of [1/h(y)] dy = integral of g(x) dx + C. The constant C on one side is enough. Example: dy/dx = xy. Separate: dy/y = x dx. Integrate: ln|y| = x^2/2 + C. Exponentiate: |y| = e^(x^2/2 + C) = Ae^(x^2/2) where A = e^C (absorb sign into A, so y = Ae^(x^2/2)).

PART 3 — Checking and writing the general solution.
After integrating, simplify and write y explicitly if possible, keeping the arbitrary constant. Verify by differentiating and substituting back. For dy/dx = xy the general solution is y = Ae^(x^2/2); check: dy/dx = Ax e^(x^2/2) = x * (Ae^(x^2/2)) = xy. Confirmed.
keyMoves: Check dy/dx factors as g(x) times h(y)
Separate: move all y-terms with dy to one side, x-terms with dx to the other
Integrate both sides and add the constant
misconceptions: Trying to separate when the right side does not factor
Forgetting the constant of integration

nodes.title: Recognise a homogeneous function
nodes.brief: Identify a homogeneous function of degree n by F(kx, ky) = k^n F(x, y).
nodes.quote: A function F(x, y) is said to be homogeneous function of degree n if
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Definition of a homogeneous function.
A function F(x, y) is called homogeneous of degree n if replacing x by kx and y by ky multiplies the function value by k^n: F(kx, ky) = k^n F(x, y) for all k. Equivalently, F(x, y) can always be written as x^n * g(y/x) for some function g. Example: F(x, y) = x^2 + xy. Check: F(kx, ky) = (kx)^2 + (kx)(ky) = k^2 x^2 + k^2 xy = k^2(x^2 + xy) = k^2 F(x, y). So degree n = 2.

PART 2 — Degree zero: the key case for DEs.
For differential equations the critical case is degree zero, where F(kx, ky) = k^0 F(x, y) = F(x, y) — the function is unchanged by the scaling. Equivalently F can be written purely as a function of y/x. Example: F(x, y) = (x^2 + y^2)/(2xy). Check with k: F(kx,ky) = (k^2 x^2 + k^2 y^2)/(2 k^2 xy) = (x^2+y^2)/(2xy) = F(x, y). Degree 0. This is exactly the type that leads to a homogeneous differential equation.

PART 3 — Quick test via the y/x rewrite.
To check if F(x, y) has degree zero, try to rewrite it as a function of v = y/x only (no x or y left separately). If you can, it is degree zero. Example: F = (x + y)/(x - y). Divide numerator and denominator by x: F = (1 + y/x)/(1 - y/x) = g(v) where v = y/x. Degree zero confirmed.
keyMoves: Replace x, y by kx, ky and factor out a power of k
The power of k is the degree n
Equivalently, write F as x^n times a function of y/x
misconceptions: Calling a non-homogeneous function homogeneous
Getting the degree wrong by miscounting the power of k

nodes.title: Solve a homogeneous differential equation
nodes.brief: Recognise dy/dx = F(x,y) homogeneous when F is degree zero; solve by the substitution y = vx.
nodes.quote: A differential equation of the form  $\frac{dy}{dx} = F(x, y)$  is said to be *homogenous* if F(x, y) is a homogenous function of degree zero.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Recognising a homogeneous DE.
A differential equation dy/dx = F(x, y) is homogeneous if F(x, y) is a homogeneous function of degree zero — equivalently F can be written purely as a function of v = y/x. Example: dy/dx = (x^2 + y^2)/(2xy). Divide numerator and denominator by x^2: F = (1 + (y/x)^2)/(2(y/x)) = (1+v^2)/(2v). This is a function of v = y/x only — degree zero confirmed.

PART 2 — The y = vx substitution.
To solve, put y = vx. Then dy/dx = v + x dv/dx (differentiate using the product rule). Substitute into the DE: v + x dv/dx = F(x, vx) = g(v) (since F is degree zero). Rearrange: x dv/dx = g(v) - v. This is separable in v and x: dv/(g(v) - v) = dx/x. Integrate both sides, then back-substitute v = y/x.

PART 3 — Worked example: dy/dx = (x + y)/x.
F = (x+y)/x = 1 + y/x = 1 + v — a function of v. Put y=vx: dy/dx = v + x dv/dx. So v + x dv/dx = 1 + v. Then x dv/dx = 1. Separate: dv = dx/x. Integrate: v = ln|x| + C. Back-substitute v = y/x: y/x = ln|x| + C, so y = x(ln|x| + C). General solution: y = x ln|x| + Cx.
keyMoves: Check F(x, y) is homogeneous of degree zero (a function of y/x)
Substitute y = vx, dy/dx = v + x dv/dx
Separate variables in v and x, integrate, then put v = y/x
misconceptions: Applying the y = vx substitution when F is not degree zero
Forgetting to back-substitute v = y/x at the end

nodes.title: Recognise a first order linear differential equation
nodes.brief: Identify the standard form dy/dx + Py = Q with P, Q functions of x only.
nodes.quote: where, P and Q are constants or functions of x only, is known as a first order linear differential equation.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Standard form of a linear first-order DE.
A first order linear differential equation has the standard form dy/dx + P(x) y = Q(x), where P and Q are constants or functions of x only. The key features: y and dy/dx each appear to the first power only; they are not multiplied together; and the coefficients P, Q involve x alone (not y). Example: dy/dx + (2/x) y = x^2 is linear with P = 2/x and Q = x^2.

PART 2 — Rearranging into standard form.
Equations are often given in non-standard arrangement. Always rearrange to isolate dy/dx on the left and group all y-terms on the left with coefficient P: move every term except dy/dx to the right, then move all y-terms to the left side. Example: x dy/dx + 2y = x^3. Divide through by x: dy/dx + (2/x) y = x^2. Now P = 2/x, Q = x^2 can be read off directly.

PART 3 — The companion form in x and y.
Sometimes it is more natural to write x as a function of y. The companion form is dx/dy + P1(y) x = Q1(y) where P1, Q1 are functions of y only. The same integrating-factor method applies with x and y roles swapped. Example: dy/dx = y/(y + x) can be rearranged to dx/dy - (1/y) x = 1 (companion form with P1 = -1/y, Q1 = 1).
keyMoves: Rearrange the equation into the form dy/dx + Py = Q
Read off P and Q as functions of x only
Recognise the companion form dx/dy + P1 x = Q1 when needed
misconceptions: Calling an equation linear when y and y' are multiplied together
Misidentifying P or Q after failing to put the equation in standard form

nodes.title: Compute the integrating factor
nodes.brief: Form the integrating factor I.F. = e^(integral of P dx) for a linear equation.
nodes.quote: This function  $g(x) = e^{\int P dx}$  is called *Integrating Factor* (I.F.) of the given differential equation.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Why an integrating factor is needed.
The left side of dy/dx + Py = Q is generally not the derivative of a product, so we cannot integrate it directly. The idea is to multiply both sides by a carefully chosen function I.F.(x) so that the left side becomes d/dx [y * I.F.]. Once in that form, integrating both sides gives the solution immediately. The function that achieves this is I.F. = e^(integral of P dx).

PART 2 — Why I.F. = e^(integral of P dx) works.
Multiply dy/dx + Py = Q by I.F.: I.F. * dy/dx + P * I.F. * y = Q * I.F. For the left side to equal d/dx[y * I.F.] = I.F. * dy/dx + y * d(I.F.)/dx, we need d(I.F.)/dx = P * I.F., i.e. (1/I.F.) d(I.F.)/dx = P. This gives d(ln I.F.)/dx = P, so ln I.F. = integral of P dx, hence I.F. = e^(integral of P dx). Example: dy/dx + (1/x) y = x. Here P = 1/x, so integral of P dx = integral of (1/x) dx = ln|x|, and I.F. = e^(ln|x|) = |x| = x (taking x > 0).

PART 3 — Computing I.F. in practice.
Step 1: identify P from the standard form dy/dx + Py = Q. Step 2: compute integral of P dx (no constant needed here). Step 3: I.F. = e^(that integral). Simplify using e^(ln u) = u. Example: P = 2x gives integral = x^2, I.F. = e^(x^2). Example: P = -3/x gives integral = -3 ln|x| = ln|x|^(-3), I.F. = e^(ln x^(-3)) = x^(-3) = 1/x^3.
keyMoves: Identify P from the standard form dy/dx + Py = Q
Compute the integral of P dx
Form I.F. = e^(integral of P dx)
misconceptions: Using Q instead of P in the integrating factor
Forgetting the exponential in I.F. = e^(integral of P dx)

nodes.title: Solve a first order linear differential equation
nodes.brief: Apply the standard steps: write standard form, find the I.F., then y(I.F.) = integral of (Q x I.F.) dx + C.
nodes.quote: Steps involved to solve first order linear differential equation:
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The three-step solution procedure.
To solve a first order linear DE: (i) Write in standard form dy/dx + P(x) y = Q(x) and identify P and Q. (ii) Compute I.F. = e^(integral of P dx). (iii) Multiply both sides by I.F.; the left side becomes d/dx[y * I.F.], so integrate both sides to get y * I.F. = integral of (Q * I.F.) dx + C. Divide by I.F. to obtain y explicitly.

PART 2 — Worked example: solve dy/dx + (1/x) y = x (x > 0).
Step (i): standard form confirmed, P = 1/x, Q = x. Step (ii): integral of (1/x) dx = ln x, so I.F. = e^(ln x) = x. Step (iii): multiply by x: x dy/dx + y = x^2, i.e. d/dx[xy] = x^2. Integrate: xy = x^3/3 + C. Divide by x: y = x^2/3 + C/x. This is the general solution.

PART 3 — Particular solution and companion form.
Apply initial condition to find C. Example: if y(1) = 2, then 2 = 1/3 + C, so C = 5/3, giving y = x^2/3 + 5/(3x). For the companion form dx/dy + P1(y) x = Q1(y), repeat the same steps with y as the independent variable: I.F. = e^(integral of P1 dy) and x * I.F. = integral of (Q1 * I.F.) dy + C.
keyMoves: Put the equation in standard form and read off P and Q
Compute the integrating factor I.F. = e^(integral of P dx)
Write y(I.F.) = integral of (Q x I.F.) dx + C and solve
misconceptions: Forgetting to multiply Q by the I.F. inside the integral
Skipping the standard-form step and misidentifying P, Q
